ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for purple rice yogurt. FOODON_03315671 (yogurt food product) used as the closest defensible anchor; purple rice grain component is not captured in this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Yoghurt) used as the closest defensible anchor. The purple rice component is not represented in FoodEx2; this is a composite dish combining fermented dairy with cooked purple glutinous rice.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for purple rice yogurt composite dish. SNOMED CT coverage for mixed grain-dairy preparations is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Original Purple Rice Yogurt is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small cup, as typically sold in Singaporean convenience stores and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: Original Purple Rice Yogurt — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Original Purple Rice Yogurt (cr_id G00010447). All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from the fat profile of plain whole-milk yogurt per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171284) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; purple rice / black rice analogous estimate; University of Sydney GI Database reference for mixed-grain yogurt preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Original Purple Rice Yogurt in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 based on: (1) plain yogurt GI of approximately 36 (University of Sydney GI Database); (2) purple/black glutinous rice GI of approximately 55–60 (peer-reviewed literature: Boontham et al., 2018, Journal of Cereal Science; anthocyanin content reduces starch digestibility vs white rice GI ~72); (3) composite dish weighted estimate given approximately 60% rice and 40% yogurt by energy contribution. Resulting blended GI estimate of 50 is conservative and classified as low (≤55). Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (50 × 25.19 × 1.5) / 100 = 18.9, rounded to 18.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g (Na:K ratio < 1.0)
hypertension.guidance: Limit serving to 150 g to keep sodium contribution to approximately 347 mg per serving; the favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (0.71) and high dietary fibre (3.7 g/100 g) are consistent with DASH diet principles, but patients with stage 2 hypertension should monitor cumulative daily sodium intake carefully.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100 g with low estimated GI (~50) and high dietary fibre (3.7 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low estimated GI (~50) and high fibre content support attenuated postprandial glucose response; however, carbohydrate at 25.2 g/100 g requires portion control — a 150 g serving delivers approximately 37.8 g carbohydrate, which should be counted within the patient's daily carbohydrate budget; confirm no added sweeteners in the yogurt component.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.8 g/100 g estimated), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (3.7 g/100 g), and anthocyanin-rich purple rice
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of negligible cholesterol (0 mg/100 g), low estimated saturated fat (~1.8 g/100 g), high dietary fibre, and purple rice anthocyanins (which have demonstrated LDL-lowering and anti-inflammatory properties in peer-reviewed studies) makes this a cardioprotective food choice when consumed in standard portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Purple rice anthocyanins and yogurt live cultures provide anti-inflammatory and gut microbiome benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Purple rice polyphenols and yogurt live active cultures may reduce systemic inflammation via antioxidant activity and gut microbiome modulation; calcium (54 mg/100 g) supports bone health relevant to RA patients on corticosteroid therapy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Original Purple Rice Yogurt is a Singaporean chilled dairy-grain composite dish consisting of tangy plain yogurt folded together with cooked chewy purple glutinous rice (also known as black rice or zimi in Chinese). The dish is sold commercially in Singapore and is associated with the 'QQ' texture descriptor — a Singaporean and Taiwanese colloquial term for a pleasantly chewy, springy mouthfeel. Purple glutinous rice is a whole-grain variety rich in anthocyanins, the same class of polyphenols found in blueberries and red cabbage, which give the rice its deep violet-purple colour and contribute antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The yogurt component provides live bacterial cultures, protein (6.5 g/100 g), and calcium (54 mg/100 g), while the purple rice contributes complex carbohydrates (25.19 g/100 g), dietary fibre (3.7 g/100 g), iron (2.55 mg/100 g), magnesium (40 mg/100 g), and potassium (327 mg/100 g). The dish is notably low in sugar (0.21 g/100 g) despite its creamy-sweet flavour profile, and has an estimated glycemic index of approximately 50 (low GI), making it a more glycaemically favourable grain-based option compared to white rice dishes. Sodium is moderate at 231 mg/100 g, offset by a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio. The dish is suitable for vegetarians, is dairy-containing, and is generally considered halal. It is commonly consumed as a snack, light lunch, or dessert in Singapore food courts and convenience stores.
common_user_queries: How many calories in purple rice yogurt?
Is purple rice yogurt good for diabetics?
What is QQ texture in food?
Is purple rice yogurt high in sugar?
Can I eat purple rice yogurt if I have high blood pressure?
Is purple rice yogurt gluten free?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010447, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed, provenance canonical). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy plain whole-milk yogurt as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; this is the only estimated field. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists for this specific product.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 171284), proportionally adjusted for the fat content of this dish (4.21 g/100 g total fat). HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific purple rice yogurt formulation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g per HPB source; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB canonical record — consistent with a low-fat dairy-grain composite where cholesterol is negligible. 4. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific commercial product was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 5. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 37.785) / 100 = 18.9, reported as 18.8 after rounding. 6. Halal status assumed based on ingredients (dairy, rice) with no porcine or alcohol-derived components identified; formal halal certification should be verified with the manufacturer.
